serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chilli Mutton as a side dish in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, representing a moderate portion of bite-sized mutton pieces in chilli sauce alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Mutton — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chilli Mutton (cr_id G00010244) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-sauce dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Mutton. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-sauce dishes with comparable carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dominant carbohydrate sources are chilli sauce and onion, both of which have low GI values. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 467 mg per 100 g, likely higher in practice due to soy sauce and chilli sauce additions
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce, and avoid adding table salt, as a 150 g serving already delivers approximately 700 mg sodium — nearly 30% of the 2400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g) and high protein (14.05 g/100g) support glycaemic stability; estimated GI is low at 35
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chilli Mutton is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 150 g portion, as the low carbohydrate load (GL ≈ 3.9) and high protein content support postprandial glycaemic control; however, soy sauce and chilli sauce may contain added sugars, so label checking is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (11.58 g/100g) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) from mutton, combined with high sodium (467 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Chilli Mutton in moderation — no more than 100–150 g per occasion — due to the combined burden of saturated fat from mutton, dietary cholesterol at 85 mg/100g, and high sodium; choosing leaner cuts and requesting reduced sauce can lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory pathways; nightshade vegetables (chilli, bell pepper, tomato) may trigger individual flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid Chilli Mutton due to the pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid profile of mutton and the potential nightshade sensitivity from chilli, bell pepper, and tomato; substituting mutton with chicken or tofu and monitoring personal flare response is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Mutton is a Singaporean dish consisting of bite-sized chunks of mutton cooked in a spiced chilli sauce, typically incorporating soy sauce, garlic, ginger, onion, tomato, and bell pepper. The dish is a staple in Singaporean hawker centres and Indian-Muslim (Mamak) stalls, reflecting the South Asian culinary influence on Singapore's multicultural food culture. The mutton is tender and richly flavoured, with a bold, savoury-spicy sauce that carries moderate heat. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, 7.44 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The sodium level is clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycemic index (≈35) make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion-controlled. Mutton's arachidonic acid content raises concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Chilli Mutton is typically served as a side dish with steamed white rice or roti prata and is consumed at lunch and dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010244) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI specifically) but the overall record confidence is high due to the exact HPB match for nutritional data.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific Chilli Mutton preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010244) as of 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate of 35 (low) based on comparable mixed meat-and-sauce dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Chilli Mutton was found in approved sources. Halal status is assumed based on the dish's prevalence in Indian-Muslim hawker stalls in Singapore; individual vendor verification is recommended. Soy sauce is listed as an allergen source (gluten-containing soy sauce is common); gluten-free soy sauce variants exist.
